title: Understand: Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights: Part II
description: Check your understanding of the modern debates over elections and voting rights with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Elections and Voting Rights: From the Founding through the Civil Rights Movement
Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights: Part II
Understand: Modern Debates over Elections and Voting Rights: Part II

assessment_items.question_content:
**In the Supreme Court, and in the country, debate about the scope of constitutional protections for the right to vote \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: ended with the passage of the Fourteenth Amendment
continues today, with ongoing discussions and legal challenges
was never really a serious debate
occurred only in the 1800s

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the best definition of *gerrymandering*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The drawing of districts to benefit a particular party or group
The attempt to influence public officials on a particular issue
The redrawing of districts following the administration of a census
The practice of politicians trading votes for each other’s legislation

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which Supreme Court case dealt with racial gerrymandering?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Shaw v. Reno*
*South Carolina v. Katzenbach*
*Rucho v. Common Cause*
*Harper v. Virginia Board of Elections*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which Supreme Court case dealt with partisan gerrymandering?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Rucho v. Common Cause*
*Baker v. Carr*
*Reynolds v. Sims*
*Shaw v. Reno*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following  is the best definition of “racial gerrymandering”?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The intentional drawing of districts to diminish the power of racial minorities
The grouping of congressional districts so that all racial minorities are represented
The complete disregard of race in drawing congressional districts
The drawing of congressional districts so that no one race is in the majority
A vote in which members of a governing body are chosen by the entire population
The drawing of districts to benefit a particular group or party
A voting system in which the first person to win a simple majority wins the election
The act of voting for all the candidates of a particular party

assessment_items.question_content:
**What doctrine emerged from the Supreme Court’s decision in *Shaw v. Reno*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There are limits to using race in the districting process.
Race can never be used as a factor in determining the drawing of congressional districts.
Racial gerrymandering is encouraged if the goal is to secure representation for minority voters.
The Supreme Court does not have the power to police racial or partisan gerrymandering claims.
The practice of drawing districts to benefit a particular political party
The intentional drawing of districts to diminish the power of racial minorities
The apportionment of representatives based on the number of votes each party received
The practice of electing members of a governing body by the entire population

assessment_items.question_content:
**What did the Supreme Court say in *Rucho v. Common Cause*? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Supreme Court does not have the power to police partisan gerrymandering claims.
Partisan gerrymandering violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
Congressional districts need to be drawn such that they are all relatively equal in size.
The Voting Rights Act of 1965 is constitutional and valid based on the Fifteenth Amendment.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What best describes the effect of the Supreme Court’s recent rulings on the constitutionality of voter ID laws?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: States may have voter ID laws.
States cannot have voter ID laws.
Only the federal government can pass voter ID laws.
Only some states are prohibited from having voter ID laws.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which Supreme Court case dealt with campaign finance regulation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Citizens United v. FEC*
*Shaw v. Reno*
*Rucho v. Common Cause*
*Baker v. Carr*

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes the Supreme Court’s ruling in *Citizens United v. FEC*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Political spending by corporations is protected speech.
Congressional district drawing cannot take race into account.
Districts should be drawn relatively equal to one another.
The Supreme Court cannot rule on partisan gerrymandering.
